anchor: Uber handed Claude Code and Cursor to 5,000 engineers, ran an internal usage leaderboard, hit 84-95% monthly adoption — and burned its entire $3.4B 2026 AI budget by April. COO Andrew Macdonald coined 'tokenmaxxing' and admitted the ROI link 'is not there yet.' Same month, Axios reported a mystery enterprise ran up ~$500M on Claude in 30 days with no usage caps, employees expensing AI calls to check the weather. And Michael Burry — yes, the 'Big Short' guy — is on record saying the AI model business is 'far too expensive, too much brute force,' that compute 'will be commoditized like internet use,' and neither Anthropic nor SpaceX is plausibly worth $1T.
angle: The 2026 enterprise AI story flipped from 'are we falling behind?' to 'why is the meter spinning faster than the value?' Three independent signals — a named COO, an anonymous $500M blowout, and the most famous bubble-caller alive — all land on the same crack: usage is exploding, governance isn't, and nobody can draw a clean line from tokens to dollars yet.
connection: An unnamed enterprise spent ~$500M on Claude in a single month with zero spending limits (employees using premium calls for trivial tasks), and Michael Burry publicly called trillion-dollar AI valuations unjustified and the model business 'too much brute force.'
reaction: This one hit close. I run about a dozen agents every morning on a cron and live in Claude Code all day across my own projects — so I watch the token meter the way I used to watch a build timer. The Uber number doesn't shock me; what shocks me is that a company with that much process let a leaderboard turn token spend into a status game and never set a cap. 'Tokenmaxxing' is the most honest phrase anyone's coined this year: it names the exact failure mode where adoption metrics go up and to the right while ROI stays flat. The scarce resource was never the tokens — it's the taste to know which task is worth spending them on.
open_questions: The $500M figure is single-sourced (Axios via an AI consultant) and the enterprise is unnamed — could be exaggerated or a one-off misconfiguration rather than a trend
Is 'no measurable ROI yet' a real economics problem or just the normal lag between adoption and restructured workflows? Uber's at 4 months in
Burry has been early-and-wrong before; bubble-calling isn't the same as being right on timing
do_not_include: Sysdig autonomous LLM cyberattack (security, adjacent to yesterday's credential post)

anchor: The enterprise software giants just split three ways on one question: can an outside AI agent touch your data? SAP quietly published API Policy v4/2026 banning third-party agents from autonomous API access entirely — funnel through SAP's own Joule or you're in breach. ServiceNow shipped Action Fabric, a mandatory tollgate every external agent must pass, priced per-action, with Claude as launch partner — JPMorgan flat-out called it 'a tax on customers using outside AI agents.' Salesforce went the opposite way, opening Summer '26 to multi-agent orchestration over MCP and A2A standards (Agentforce now at $800M ARR, +169% YoY). Block, meter, or open — pick one.
angle: This is the cloud-vs-on-prem fork of the agent era. Whoever owns the integration layer decides whether AI agents are first-class citizens inside the enterprise stack or toll-paying visitors — and the pricing model (per-seat is dead, per-action is here) follows from that choice. Bain pegs the prize at a $100B cross-system labor market that's 90% uncaptured, which is exactly why every platform is racing to own the gate.
connection: SAP's restriction policy (The Register) and Salesforce's open MCP/A2A approach (Salesforce newsroom) bracket the strategy spectrum; Bain's research frames why the gate is worth fighting over — a $100B labor-to-software conversion that current vendors capture only $4-6B of.
reaction: I spent years building API integrations against enterprise systems — 120+ endpoints on a FinTech platform — and the thing nobody outside that world feels is how much an agent is just a very fast, very tireless API consumer. So SAP's policy isn't a footnote; it's a wall. When I wire one of my own agents into a third-party system, the first question now isn't 'can it authenticate' — it's 'will this vendor let an autonomous caller in at all, and what's the per-action bill when it does.' Per-seat pricing assumed a human bottleneck. Agents removed the bottleneck, so the meter moved to the action. That's the whole game, and most builders haven't priced it in yet.
open_questions: Will SAP's hard block hold, or get walked back under customer pressure the way restrictive API policies usually do?
Per-action pricing sounds clean but could get adversarial fast — what stops a platform from inflating 'actions' the way some clouds inflate egress?
Is Salesforce's openness real architectural conviction or just a temporary land-grab before it adds its own tollgate?
do_not_include: SaaSpocalypse stock reversal (saved as its own topic)

anchor: The 'SaaSpocalypse' is over at the index level — and the way it ended tells you who the AI buildout actually pays. The IGV software ETF rose 21% in May, its best month since October 2001 (Snowflake +46% in two sessions, Okta +30% on an earnings beat). But the basket is wildly split: DigitalOcean +227% YTD, Datadog +76%, CrowdStrike +55% — while HubSpot sits at -46%, Monday.com -45%, Klaviyo -52%, even with HubSpot posting 23% revenue growth and 83%+ gross margins. Infrastructure and security — the picks-and-shovels — are winning. Seat-priced application software is being left for dead.
angle: The market just priced a thesis builders have felt for a year: when agents do the work, you don't buy more seats, so software valued per-human-login deflates while software valued per-unit-of-compute-or-protection inflates. Strong fundamentals (HubSpot's growth and margins) can't save you if your pricing model assumes a human in every chair.
connection: CNBC documents the index-level recovery (best month since 2001, though IGV is still -3.8% YTD vs Nasdaq +18%), giving the macro frame for the uneven, name-by-name divergence SaaStr breaks down.
reaction: As someone who's shipped three solo products this year, the HubSpot data point is the one I keep staring at: 23% growth, 83% gross margins, and still down 46%. That's not a broken company — that's a broken pricing assumption. The market is telling app-layer builders the per-seat model is a melting ice cube, and it's rewarding the layer that sells compute, storage, and security by the unit instead. I think about this every time I design how one of my own tools should charge: the second your product's value scales with agent actions instead of human logins, your old pricing page is already obsolete.
open_questions: Is the infra/app split structural, or just a rotation that mean-reverts once app-layer companies re-price to consumption?
DigitalOcean +227% is eye-popping — how much is AI thesis vs a low-base bounce or buyout speculation?
Could 'seat-based is dead' be overstated — plenty of software still has a human bottleneck that agents haven't touched
do_not_include: Enterprise agent-access tollgate story (its own topic)
